summary: Gruber frames the Apple Watch as the natural evolution of the digital watch, drawing a line from James Bond's gadget-laden Seikos and Pulsars of the 1970s to Apple's modern wrist computer. He argues that critics asking 'Do you need an Apple Watch?' are posing the wrong question — no one needs any $400 watch. The right question is whether you want one, just as it was with the iPad. He acknowledges the interaction model is complex but argues that complexity is inherent to the feature set, not a design failure. The piece positions Apple Watch at the inflection point where computer-y gadgets become gadget-y computers, consistent with Apple's historical pattern with iPod and iPhone.
key_points: Apple Watch is fundamentally a digital watch reimagined for the era of ubiquitous wireless networking and miniaturized computing
The 'Do you need it?' framing is the wrong question — watches are about desire, convenience, and style, not necessity, just as iPad critics learned
The interaction complexity is justified by the feature density, analogous to the inscrutable but accepted interfaces of 1980s Casio digitals
Apple enters product categories at the tipping point where Moore's Law allows a gadget-y computer to outcompete computer-y gadgets
The Bond watch lineage — from Connery's Rolex to Moore's gadget-laden digitals — presaged exactly what Apple Watch delivers: function and style on the wrist
money_quotes: This is what Apple Watch is: an ambitious modern take on the digital watch.
Much of the criticism of Apple Watch is being driven by the question 'Do you need an Apple Watch?' And that is simply the wrong question.
Of course you don't need it. No one, not one person on the face of the earth, needs any $400 watch, Apple Watch or otherwise.
The right question is simply 'Do you want one?'
It's about desire, not necessity. Convenience, fun, and style are not needs. They're wants. And people will gladly pay for what they want.
It's a cool watch that does cool things.
Apple Watch is not hard to understand fundamentally. It's just a digital watch, reimagined for today's world.
This is the watch my teenage 1987 self would have expected my 2015 self to own.
key_insight: Apple Watch doesn't need to justify its existence through necessity — like all watches, it's about want, not need, and the real achievement is delivering Bond-movie fantasies in a stylish, functional package.
